[IMAGE:0]It's right on midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books on magic these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book on the left is the current edition of my fourth published book, Earth Divination, Earth Magic. This was the first of two books I've written so far on geomancy, an intriguing form of divination using sixteen four-digit binary numbers -- okay, they're combinations of one and two dots, but the mathematics are binary. It was one of the most common methods of divination in the Middle Ages and Renaissance, but dropped almost entirely out of use thereafter, and important parts of the system got lost. I had to learn Latin and find a couple of rare texts --one of which is translated in this book -- to figure them out. This book originally came out from one of the big Neopagan publishers, and their marketing people apparently didn't bother to read it; they pushed it on Pagan bookstores claiming that it was some sort of Wiccan feng-shui, and of course most of the copies got returned to them. It was the first of my books to go out of print. Fortunately it (and my other book on geomancy -- that's a story all by itself, which we'll get to in due time) found their audience despite the misplaced efforts of its original publisher, and it's now selling steadily with a different press.  If you're interested, you can get a copy here if you live in the US and here if you're anywhere else.)

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

comments.title: Five Rites Review and Question
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Well, despite my intuition that fall is obviously for the dead and spring for the living, it appears not everyone shares that, so if you mark it, I hope everyone had an appropriate Veterans' Day.

To Share: I've written a quick piece on my thoughts on our host's newest book on the Five Rites: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_secret_of_the_five_rites

To Ask: This might be a project for down the road, but I figured I'd ask while it's on my mind. So far, I have found the Five Rites fully compatible with DMH/DA work, invigorating to the physical and subtle bodies, and convenient enough to practice regularly with a busy schedule, so I'm pretty happy with them. That being said, I also like to tinker, and given my current focus n work with the Three Cauldrons (plus the Solar Plexus) from DMH/DA work, I found myself wondering about physical exercises that might consciously seek to stimulate the belly, heart, and "third eye" energy centers, since only the last is discussed in the Five Rites system. I thought maybe some of the Hindu texts discussing lesser chakras you mentioned in The Secret of the Five Rites might be helpful to look into for pursuing this - any particular place you recommend I start?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who will have them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Five Rites Review and Question
replies.content:
I spent much of the time I was doing research for The Secret of the Five Rites trying to find good sources in English or French on the minor chakras, and ended up with very, very little -- just enough information to identify which minor chakras the designers of the Five Rites used, and annoying references to detailed volumes in Sanskrit. If anyone else can recommend a good source I'd be delighted to hear it!

The Daily Dozen, interestingly enough, is also something I've worked with and had good results with; I included it in one of my most obscure works, The Spirit and the Sword, and may do something one of these days explaining how to use it as a system of esoteric exercise and training. These days, though, I'm working mostly with the Rites.

replies.title: Re: Five Rites Review and Question
replies.content:
Mantak Chia's books are a good reference in English from a familiar field to you, but it is not exhaustive about energy centers, though it has a great tip about redirecting awakened energy safely to avoid headaches etc. from odd energetic interactions.

A more medical take can be found on Acupuncture and the Chakra Energy System and has some of the minor chakras.

comments.title: Cosmic eclipse update
comments.content:
What is your current take on the 'cosmic eclipse' theory, as in the speculation that behind the madness of the last three years was a solar system on another plane blocking our access to the central sun. I think you said once that if it was blocking on the second or third cosmic plane it would have a more dramatic but less long lasting effect.

For what it's worth, I think the eclipse is fading now, as that horrible gnawing straining feeling of not knowing which way was "up" has passed. Well it has for me.

However it hasn't been replaced with a sense of joy either, and I wonder if that's the collective karma of how so many of us behaved during the 'eclipsed times'.

I would be keen to get your take.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic eclipse update
replies.content:
It seems so far to be matching up rather nicely with the facts. I note that a lot of people who were shrill advocates of police-state tactics toward unvaccinated people are now not merely backing away from that stance, but insisting that they never actually said anything of the kind -- and I don't think it's just dishonesty or cognitive dissonance. At least some of them really do believe it, and get a deer-in-the-headlights look when you show them their previous tweets and emails.

But it's fair to say that the "eclipse" may be over but the karma remains. Now to see how that plays out...

replies.title: Re: Cosmic eclipse update
replies.content: Why would the eclipse prevent people from realizing what they had done/said?

replies.title: Re: Cosmic eclipse update
replies.content: State-dependent memory is a common human trait -- it's easier for you to remember events when you're in the same emotional state you were when they happened. This is why all those miserable events from the past inevitably come up when you're depressed, for example. Since the impact of the 2nd or 3rd plane solar system will have pulled the weak-minded into a mental and emotional state wholly alien to them -- and to humanity in general! -- now that the influence is passing off, the state they were in when they said and did abusive things is just as alien to them, and so the inverse of state-dependent memory makes it easy for them to forget what they did. It won't change the karma, of course.

I wonder if this is what's driving the insistence on both the right and left that the other side has gone crazy over the last 15 odd years: the left is still resonating with the Plutonian, but the right isn't as much anymore.

The left rightly sees the right as having changed; the right sees the left as crazy but can't remember they used to be in the same weird state....

comments.title: 3rd Person and Death in Scryings
comments.content:
Happy Monday!

All of the scrying I've done was first person, with me interacting with the images I observe. In cases where I might want to scry a scene in myth which contains violence and death -- where I don't want to be the killer or the killed -- is there an issue with being a 3rd party viewer?

And then extending from that, if there were situations in a first person scrying where I (or my avatar or whatever) was killed, would that be a problem? Or is that just interpreted through the Druidic concept of rebirth and so forth? To simplify the question: should death be avoided in scryings where possible, or just treated as another symbol?


Thanks!

replies.title: Re: 3rd Person and Death in Scryings
replies.content: It's never been a problem for me, any more than it's a problem to be killed in a dream -- that's happened to me quite a few times. Just keep in mind that death is a transition and not an end, and see where it takes you.

replies.title: Re: 3rd Person and Death in Scryings
replies.content:
In any kind of visualized working, there can be great power in allowing dark shadowy malevolences that show up to attack your first-person character without defending and resisting against them, since those threats often reflect disowned aspects of the Self that have been projected safely away into one's Shadow. Our habitual knee-jerk impulse to fend them off usually stops them from being able to reintegrate back into the larger Self. Very often, as in so many mythic stories, they will appear to subsume, engulf, or destroy the heroic self, only for that self to then reappear stronger and more whole after that deathlike experience. So many stories about trips to the underworld or getting swallowed by a whale or dragon only to emerge victorious tell this tale of self discovery. Gandalf's struggling plummet with the Balrog is a modern version of the same growth.

On the other hand, some dark shadowy malevolences in one's visualized workings are exactly what they appear to be, dangerous threats to one's integrity and balance. Staying vigilantly defended against their treacherous attempts to gain entry to one's Self in order to work their destabilizing mischief is paramount for any occult practitioner. Learning to pick up on the subtle cues as to which shadow images are in fact one's own and which are wholly other is a large part of what worthwhile occult training is all about. Those two darknesses' energy signatures have distinctively different textures, yet it can take years of rigorous practice just to be able to get past the reflexive terror they inspire, so that one can then confidently recognize those differences and respond appropriately.

Death is always a symbol. The question is whether it happens to be acting as a symbol of the pathway to rebirth or as a symbol of the danger of disintegration. Or as both. Or as neither. Death really is an extraordinarily fertile symbol.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: 3rd Person and Death in Scryings

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may. This is an important issue to untangle, and for me it has gradually been untangling itself through the practice of the martial art of taichi. I would like to emphasise that many of the lessons I have taken here are from treating this training as a way to "train the mind/body, not inform it" - ie there are metaphors that come from these physical practices that extend (IMHO) upwards through the planes.

I do not know how yoga stacks up to this, but if you learn a martial art, you are mostly learning how to *prevent* an act of violence taking place against your own person (at least in the form of a physical attack from another person). As JMG says in his comment "if your non-violence encourages people to be violent, you are partly responsible for their violence." Another way to think of this is that your martial art is teaching you to BE responsible for the safety and security of your person FROM the violence of others. It is first, a way of being responsible.

At the same time, the Chen lineage, whose tai chi style I am learning, has a Chen Tai Chi written manual which contains several injunctions against a tai chi practitioner practicing violence against others because of the way this can do internal damage to the tai chi practitioner (ie not because of the damage it might do to others). Again, a matter which stresses one's personal responsibility for one's own actions

It may be that it is necessary to think carefully about what "violence" actually means. Is "violence" indistinguishable from (say) "confrontation", as your post suggests? Or can a confrontation be conducted in a way that minimises the possibility of violence? Which a martial art actually strives to train a person to be able to do.

I wonder (and perhaps our host might advise more knowledgeably) if it is not possible to construct a vow (should one really want to do such a thing) around the idea of doing one's utmost to prevent violence, and around the idea of protecting oneself and others - including the violent person themself - from the effects of violence.

On this last score, I am minded of a story I heard from someone I know who works in a shelter for homeless men. On this particular evening, a resident turned up in and advanced state of inebriation/intoxication, waving a knife in a menacing way, and generally looking extremely dangerous. While everyone else sought something to hide behind, one unarmed warden looked him in the eye, and in a clear voice, said, "Are you having a bad day? Want to tell me about it?" This brought the man down into a much less dangerous looking place, and he gradually calmed. The man himself was prevented from DOING harm (and thus doing internal damage to himself), and also no harm came to any other person. To me, this was an instance of a fully confrontational type of non-violence, that brought a specific violent threat to an end.

There is a lot of meditation fodder here, as I am sure all, including our host, will agree.

replies.title: Wrath and meekness
replies.content:
This reminds me of an article (sorry, on paper, cannot share) on the Passion of Wrath as opposed to the Vice of Wrath. Quoting directly from Plato, the article described how Wrath is ultimately the desire to redress injustice committed against the self. While there are many ways to mitigate this passion in a positive way (by example, by noticing that the other person is acting out of unawareness instead of malfeasance) the incapacity of taking vengeance does not suppress the wrath, just creates deep sadness for the ill committed against oneself and leads to resentment.

In this sense, Wrath is a passion that can be a vice or not, depending if it is/isn't fair and proportional to the damage received. When it turns to vice it is because a lack or reason to properly rein in the passion and direct it appropriately.

The virtue that conquers Wrath is Meekness; but in the purest Aristotelian style, every vice sits in the golden middle between to vices of opposing qualities. Meekness can be not "lack of violence", because there are injustices in this world that can only be resisted by violent means, and failing to defend oneself against injustice is a vice called Faint-heartedness.

On the other hand, the word Meekness comes from Mansuetude whose roots are Man (hand) suescere (be habituated): Habituated (to defend) with the hand. This is the same idea behind the Japanese word Karate: Kara (bare) te (hand), as opposed to a hand bearing sword. This is emphatically not non-violence, but constrained violence, as guided by discipline and reason.

This question references two themes I have seen repeated here. I'm referencing them as they appeared, even though my question is a general one rather than specific.

The first theme: if you do a candle working (or planetary charity) for earning money, it is important to take any opportunity to earn money that presents itself.

The second theme: the key to getting wealthy is to look at everything as an opportunity to make money.

I worked for someone who had done the second, with great success: he'd grown up very poor, and was wealthy. He was not someone I would want to become - his willingness to scam others to earn money was legendary.

So the overall question: is is possible to do a candle working for some sort of opportunity, but specify limits on it, without offending the planetary intelligences? "I'd like an opportunity to earn money by honest work", for example.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Candle working: opportunities within limits?
replies.content: This is a very important point. Of course you should put limits on any such working -- everyone does, at least implicitly. "Opportunities to get money" could be taken to include robbing at gunpoint every person you meet! The intention you've defined is a good way to do things -- you're phrasing it positively, not negatively, and so asking the intelligence to work in a specific direction. They can handle that very well.

replies.title: Re: Candle working: opportunities within limits?
replies.content:
Thank you!

SamChevre

comments.title: Bardon and other stuff
comments.content:
Good day to you Sage

I wish you and your loved ones health and strength in a time of shorter days and longer nights!


1. what do you think about combining the practices from Bardon (introduction to Hermeticism) with the druidic golden dawn from your books?

2 I was also wondering how the practices from Bardon combine with Tai chi quan, do you have any opinion on this?

3. do you think it is possible to remake the practices from Bardon's Introduction to Hermeticism in pagan fashion? Perhaps someone has already done this?
I realize that Hermeticism by nature is universal, but further it doesn't hurt to ask! :D

replies.title: Re: Bardon and other stuff
replies.content:
I can sum up my responses to all three questions with one very simple statement:

Don't mix or modify practices until you know them well enough to do it right.

Magical practices aren't necessarily compatible; some work well together, others produce a range of nasty consequences, and still others cancel each other out. Nor is it at all obvious what the issues are unless you've actually done the work and understand it from within. Thus if you want to combine Bardon's practices with The Celtic Golden Dawn, first complete the work in Bardon's book exactly as written; then complete the work of The Celtic Golden Dawn exactly as written -- and then you'll know both well enough to be able to figure out how they do or don't fit together.

The same is true of both of your other questions. Master the systems as given, setting aside the urge to tinker with them until you know how they work, and then -- once you can do so from a basis of personal knowledge -- you can decide what tinkerings you want to explore.

There's an important issue here. Tinkering with practices you haven't yet done is a way of defending yourself against new experiences, new insights, and new ways of understanding the world. The ego gets very defensive when its boundaries are threatened, and no wonder: it really is scary to encounter the world in some genuinely new and original way! Scary or not, though, if we don't stop clutching our ideas and approach new teachings from a place of openness, we remain locked away in the narrow little prison of our existing ideas.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings everyone!

This is for JMG but open to everyone for comments.

Given what's going on in the Middle East, the New Yorker released an interview with a settler that was suitably crazy (including stating out loud they want a greater Israel from the nile to euphretes), which is normal for normally fringe religionists but the exception here is that these people are close to parts of the Israeli right wing govt etc.

I keep tabs on the the far left and far right on twitter and I see more extremism lately, including open anti-semitism, calls for racial identity to be organised around, more rhetoric about a return to a "Christian America" (something I looked into and realised was a fabrication particularly the new Deal era).

You're also seeing some type of "second religiosity" with nick land, paul kingsnorth, and now Ayaan Hirsi Ali claiming to find "Christianity" and revert to the "judeo-christian tradition" that the modern west is based on (again a convenient distortion of what actually happened).

This whole line on thinking alarms me because I think it is disingenuous and operating out of bad faith, in that it is a political device to make the west more manageable again (though I am open to be proven wrong on this).

When I use Steiner 3 way model of luciferic pride and arhimanic materality which can only be balanced by the Christ heart centre or humility, I theorised that such intense religious fundamentalism or ethnic extremism is a type of delusion created by an imbalance towards the head (luciferic pride that causes a lack of grounding and an aggressive expansionist superiority complex that at its extreme gets murderous), and is a development that is responding to the arhimanic materialism for the recent past, but either way we don't get a balance.

It seems to me that for people with psycho-emotional imbalances, spirituality can make things worse.

My question is what is happening spiritually for such kind of extremism and even general second religiosity reversion to manifest itself? What is the spiritual nature of fundamentalism and radicalization among those who claim to walk in god's light?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Okay, this is a complex question and it has no simple answer. There's a lot going on that feeds into the current mess!

To begin with, there's a standard historical process at work. Every civilization has an age of reason, and every age of reason comes to an end because rationalism never really works. As an age of reason ends, you always get intellectuals fleeing back to the former established religion of the civilization. They don't do it for spiritual reasons -- they do it because their faith in reason has collapsed and they need a comfortable, familiar ideology to take its place and provide a bulwark against mental chaos. That's what Land, Kingsnorth, and Ali have done, and there will be many more; Oswald Spengler called this process the Second Religiosity. Our civilization is in the early stages of it.

Second, Ahrimanic and Luciferic influences alternate within our civilization in a two-century cycle. The 20th century was Ahrimanic, the 19th century was Luciferic, the 18th was Ahrimanic, and so on. Thus we're moving into a Luciferic cycle and people are reacting accordingly.

Finally, the United States is in deep trouble as a nation, and even more so as a global hegemon. In any nation in crisis, people tend to flock to extreme belief systems; that's driving both the extreme religious vibe and the extreme wokester vibe these days.

That's my take, at least.

replies.content:

I presume you don't take Steiner's view on a spiritual war on-going on the higher planes with St Michael as the leader, a soon due incarnation of arhiman, and the etheric second coming of christ literally? Really don't know how to evaluate these claims myself.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
I learned it by studying history. I don't have a spiritual tradition's backing for it -- I simply noticed that centuries alternate: the Ahrimanic nature of the 20th century is hard to miss, but so is the Luciferic arrogance and spiritual pride of Victorian culture; then we've got the Georgian era, which was a time of spectacular corruption and sexual excess; then we've got the Puritan era in the 17th century, the bawdy Elizabethan era in the 16th, and so on back. It's quite a remarkable rhythm.

No, I don't take Steiner's view in this. As I noted in a blog post on the man --

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-perils-of-the-pioneer/

-- he was brilliant but he was venturing into brand new territory with his clairvoyant explorations, and so he got some things wrong.

While a civilization lurches forward into decline and fall, of which the second religiosity is a symptom, something else is going on under the surface: the core religious metaphors of the next civilization are sprouting. Just as Christianity emerged in the husk of the Roman world and both Buddhism and religious Taoism found circles of believers during the collapse of China's Han dynasty, the new religious, spiritual, and occult movements of our time are groping toward the new spirituality of an age not yet born.

comments.title: Dementia, Death, and Energy Drain
comments.content:
My mother's sister, age 77, has had dementia for a few years now and two weeks ago we got the call that she was declining. My aunt was moved into hospice of the Memory Care facility that she is at.

On Friday, we arrived at the hospice facility to say our goodbyes and my Aunt, who has not eaten since Sept 27th and has only taken some water and gatorade, did not seem on the edge of death but instead was withered, pale, gray in complexion, and full of angry energy.

The doctors and nurses seem to think she will pass any day, but she does not seem to be so close to me. I will spare everyone the details, but my cousin, who is closest in geography and always with my aunt, and her husband came down with Covid two weeks ago though my Aunt did not.

When we arrived my cousin and her daughter were drained and exhausted. My Aunt was feisty, thought my Mom was herself and didn't remember me.

After we departed to return to my brother's house where my mom and I were staying, I nearly fell asleep at dinner and was so exhausted that I went to bed four hours before my normal bedtime. On Saturday, the same happened to my mom but mid-day and she is not an afternoon napper.

Yesterday both of us had very odd digestive troubles and pains on the 540 mile drive back to our town. As we were stopped at the Dairy Queen in the middle of the desert in Ludlow, Calif for the restrooms, I turned to my mom and said, "I think that there was a spiritual parasite at the hospice center who is feeding off the fear of death."

What I didn't say to my mom, as to not upset her, was that I thought that either my aunt or cousin was draining the energy off of us for the short two hours we had been at the hospice center. For both of us, spending more than 40 mins in the bright sun walking the dog around the Dairy Queen parking lot did quite a bit to restore us.

I spoke to a knowledgeable friend who thinks it was my Aunt is not ready to die even though she is not eating and is extending her life by draining our energy and that of other family members.

Has our host or any of the commentariat heard of or experienced the above? Do you know more about this? Any resources?

Thank you.

Many years ago I worked as a nurse's aide in nursing homes, and in every place I worked, the aides knew which patients were vampires and would avoid spending time around them, because they would drain your energy. There are various ways to deal with it; Dion Fortune's Psychic Self-Defence covers some of them.

Keep in mind, though, that one of the core reasons that many people get sick and die quickly once they're moved into a nursing home is that nursing homes, whatever they promise, inevitably provide the absolute legal minimum of care for their residents. The nurses and aides do the best they can, but they're always loaded with too many people to take care of -- that's profitable for corporate, after all -- and so the residents always end up being neglected. Lying there in bed all alone for hours at a time with nothing to look forward to but more of the same until you die is a great recipe for depression and collapsing health.

comments.title: Maintenance vs Development
comments.content:
Hello JMG, et. all,


After reading through The Way of the Golden Section, having a great deal of fun doing the drawing exercises, and finally feeling like I have an understanding of why Pi is so often used to solve equations (something no math teacher ever managed to accomplish) I was excited to try out the oracle cards. With my current trajectory of spiritual development as my focus, I drew card 20 R; Excess, for where I am now. And card 20; Expansion, for what I should avoid.
A very clear message of: "Please stop doing the same things over and over" Fair enough.
(I drew card 22 R; Imperfection, for what I should embrace, so I can confirm that there are other cards in the deck.)


I’ve also recently found myself drawn towards the Essene Healing Work - tried the Blessing Walk and enjoyed it - so I consulted the cards to see if this work would be aligned with my goals. I drew, yet gain, card 20 R; Excess. At this point I reached for my tarot deck for clarity/confirmation and got 4 of Wands; Completion.
“Stop doing the same things over and over”, and “Yes, you’ve already done this”.


Now, this was incredibly frustrating for me. Part of the reason I feel drawn to the Essene work is because I'm acutely aware of the absence of anything like it in my current life. Being connected to that universal healing energy feels like a gulp of fresh air in an otherwise smoggy world.
Stewing over this situation while out for a walk I eventually stopped to make a plea to my Higher Self/ Guardians, "If this is work I've already completed and I’m not to pursue it any further…then let me draw from it and replenish myself, at least!" I then proceed through the Blessing Walk visualizations and, boy howdy! what a difference! It was electric, and joyous; my whole body tingled and vibrated. I felt so alive!

This leads me to my question: How does one interact with a magical system from a maintenance standpoint, instead of a developmental one? To what extent can techniques be utilized -if at all- without developing what no longer needs any further development?
The advice I got from the cards was: card 13; Cooperation. "Seek an outside perspective." Hence the question asking :)


Lastly, as irritating as much of this was, getting confirmation that my magical workings have thus far been throttled (lovingly, compassionately) was quite validating. A somewhat sizable amount of confusion has been lifted from me, which I am so very thankful for.

replies.title: Re: Maintenance vs Development
replies.content: It's when magic settles into a maintenance level that a lot of the real work gets done. Simply settle down to the basic practices for a while -- a protective ritual, a daily meditation, and a daily divination -- and leave it at that. Adding more and more things courts burnout, and it can also distract you from slow, steady work on the basics.

replies.title: Re: Maintenance vs Development
replies.content: I wonder if this practice that appeals to you so much is related to your natal Lunar South Node in astrology: the "been there, done that, got the T-shirt" aspect of reincarnation which is comfortably too easy for you now, so your best growth in this life is to pursue the opposite until you can integrate them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's considerably more complex than that. It's been suggested that some of the political arrangements of east coast tribes were deliberately put in place to make it impossible for powerful leaders to impose kingships like those of the Mississippi Valley peoples. replies.title: Re: Question, and Five Rites
replies.content:
1) It's just an attempt to get people to consider something other than cards. A lot of diviners these days tend to be card deck monomaniacs.

2) Vortices! Does Hoff do lukewarm water baths? I thought he was one of the heirs of the old cold-water tradition.

replies.title: Re: Question, and Five Rites
replies.content:
Yes, Hof is cold water, though in some of his instructional material it's got more to do with discomfort than temperature, I think. I'm not much of a follower of his, though I do turn my showers to cold at the end most of the time in a nod to it. Of course, I'm on well water, so days like today when the weather is near freezing, I don't turn the shower all the way to cold.

replies.title: Re: Question, and Five Rites
replies.content: Hydrotherapy aka "the water cure" wasn't limited to occultism -- it was a very common form of health treatment until the Second World War. I'm pretty sure Hof's roots are in the work of Vincenz Preissnitz and Sebastian Kneipp, who pioneered cold water shock therapy in the 19th century. He may not know that that's where his roots are -- he may have picked up some ideas from later water-cure practitioners, who were inspired by Preissnitz and Kneipp.

replies.title: Re: Question, and Five Rites
replies.content:
Not the OP, but on 2), very much cold! Or, as Wim calls it "My warm friend!"

Unless he has introduced something new since I worked through his course ~7 years ago, his only use for cool/tepid baths or showers was as a stepping stone to getting acclimated to cold, or as the "best you can do" if you can't manage a truly cold shower or ice bath. For his retreats, he has students swim in snowmelt rivers and climb above the snowline of mountains in their shorts, so I don't think he's trying to avoid the shock to the system cold water can bring.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Dream Topography
